paragraphs.text: Having finished the account of the creation on the sixth day of the creeping things, cattle, beasts, and man, and of their blessing by God, Moses writes that God rested on the seventh day, and says: “Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them… And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made” (Gen 2:1–2). After what toil did God rest? Behold, what was created on the first day was created by a single nod, except for the light alone, which was created by a word; and all the rest of the creatures on the following days were created by a single word. Who shall say that God was wearied by uttering a single word in a day, when to pronounce a single word in a day is no labor even for us? If Moses was not wearied when he divided the sea by a word and a rod, if Joshua son of Nun was not wearied when by a word he stopped the courses of the heavenly lights, could God have been wearied who by a single word created the seas and the lights?

Therefore God blessed and sanctified the seventh day not because He had need of rest (for He does not weary), nor only that He might give it to the Hebrew people for rest from labor (for once they had been freed from bondage, they did not distinguish days). God gave the seventh day so that servants, even against the will of their masters, might have rest; and at the same time, by a temporal Sabbath given to a passing nation, He wished to present the image of the true Sabbath which will be in the world without end. Moreover, since it was necessary to establish weeks of days, God magnified with a blessing that day which was not glorified by the works of creation, that by the honor given to it through this blessing it might be made equal with the other days, and the sevenfold number of days needed for the world might be filled out.

Having spoken of the Sabbath rest and of how God blessed and sanctified the seventh day, Moses turns again to recount the original ordering of the creatures; here, briefly recalling what he had already said, he expounds at length what had been left unsaid.

Beginning to repeat the account of the creation of things, he says: “These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day that the Lord God made the earth and the heavens, and every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew: for the Lord God had not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man to till the ground. But there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground” (Gen 2:4–6). Anyone hearing this should understand that, although Scripture has already spoken of the days of creation, of the sanctification and blessing of the Sabbath day, yet even after the days of creation have ended, it again turns to the account of the beginning of creation: “These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth” — that is, the account of the creation of heaven and earth — “in the day that the Lord God made the earth and the heavens.” There was as yet no plant of the field; no herb of the field had yet sprung up. But although these did not indeed appear on the first day, because they came forth on the third, yet not in vain has the word about what was created on the third day been inserted into the account of the creation of the first day. For it is then explained why the plants and herbs had not sprung up — namely, because the Lord had not sent rain upon the earth. “But there went up a fountain from the earth, and watered the whole face of the ground.” Since everything was born, as it is born even now, from the joining of waters with earth, Moses wished here to show that the plants and herbs were not created together with the earth, for rain had not yet fallen. But when the great fountain of the great deep went forth and watered the whole earth, then, after the gathering of the waters on the third day, the earth on that same day brought forth every plant.

Therefore the waters over which the darkness was spread on the first day were the very ones that came out of this fountain, and in the blink of an eye covered the whole earth. This same fountain opened up also in the days of Noah, and covered with water all the mountains that are on the earth. This fountain came forth not from under the earth, but out of the earth itself, for it is not said that it came from under the earth, but “there went up” out of the earth. That the waters which were on the earth are not older than the earth, the earth itself bears witness, for it carries them in its bosom. So, Scripture says, from the earth a fountain went up and watered “the whole face of the ground,” and then the earth produced plants, herbs, and growths. This was done not because God could not otherwise have brought forth plants from the earth, but because it pleased Him that the earth should bring forth plants with the help of the waters, and He laid down the beginning of this arrangement so that the same should continue to the end.

Having spoken of what was omitted and not set forth in the account of the creation of the first day, Moses moves on to describe the making of man, and says: “and there was no man to till the earth” (Gen 2:5) — that is, before the sixth day there was no man, because he was created on the sixth day. “And God formed man in the sixth day, dust (taken) from the ground, and breathed into his face the breath of life: and man became a living soul” (Gen 2:7). The animals, cattle, and birds at their very creation received body and soul together. But God honored man in many things: first, by creating him, as is said, with His own hand; by breathing the soul into him; by giving him authority over paradise and over all that is outside paradise; by clothing him in glory; and by giving him the gift of speech, reason, and the knowledge of the Godhead.

Speaking of the glorious making of man, Moses turns to the account of paradise and of man’s being brought into it, and says: “And the Lord God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there he put the man whom he had formed” (Gen 2:8). Eden is the place of paradise. It is said “in the beginning,”* because God planted paradise on the third day, and this is explained by the words: “And out of the ground made the Lord God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food”; and to show that the narrative is here precisely of paradise, it is added: “the tree of life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil” (Gen 2:9).

Having spoken of paradise — on what day it was planted, of the bringing of man into it, of the tree of life and of the other tree — Moses turns to the account of the river that went out of paradise and was divided “into four heads” outside paradise, and says: “And a river went out of Eden to water the garden” (Gen 2:10). Here too Moses calls the blessed land of paradise Eden. If the river had not watered paradise, it would not have been divided “into four heads” outside paradise. But the four streams that went out from the river were not, in the taste of their waters, like the original fountain. If even in our lands, which are all under a curse, the waters are different, how much more must the blessed land of Eden have differed in quality from the land subjected to the curse of the Righteous One for Adam’s transgression of the commandment.

The four streams were these: Pishon — this is the Danube; Gihon — this is the Nile; the Tigris and the Euphrates, between which we dwell (Ephrem the Syrian means his own monastery, in which he labored ascetically). Although we know the places from which these rivers flow, the origin of the spring is unknown, because paradise was on a very great height. Near paradise these rivers are swallowed up and descend into the sea, as out of some high reservoir, and passing through the earth beneath the sea, they pour forth: the first in the west, Gihon in the south, the Euphrates and the Tigris in the north.

Having spoken of paradise and of the rivers that go out from it, Moses turns to the account of Adam’s being brought into paradise and of the law given to him: “And the Lord God took the man… and put him into the garden of pleasure, to till it and to keep it” (Gen 2:15). But with what could Adam till, when he had no tools? And what need was there for him to till, when in paradise there were no thorns and thistles? How could he guard paradise, when he could not fence it round? Whom was he to guard it from, when there was no thief who might enter it? The keeping of paradise after the transgression of the commandment bears witness that, while the commandment was kept, there was no need of a watchman. Therefore on Adam was laid no other keeping than that of the law given him, no other tilling than the fulfilling of the commandment given him. If anyone says that these two tasks were laid upon Adam together with the commandment, I do not contradict him either.

Speaking of Adam’s being brought into paradise and the reason he was brought there, Moses turns to the account of the law that was given to Adam: “And the Lord God commanded Adam, saying: Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat: but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die” (Gen 2:16–17). The commandment is light, for God gave Adam the whole of paradise, but forbade him to eat the fruits of one tree alone. If a single tree had supplied man with food while many others had been forbidden him, that tree would have served him as a help in need, as food in hunger. But since, in place of the one tree which would have been enough for him, God gave him many, the actual transgression came not from need, but from negligence. God therefore forbade man only one tree, and fenced it about with the fear of death, that if man should not keep the commandment for love of the One who gave it, then the fear of death surrounding the tree might restrain him from transgressing the commandment.

Having spoken of Adam’s being brought into paradise and of the commandment given him, Moses turns to the account of how Adam named the animals, and says: “And out of the ground God also formed every beast of the field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam to see what he would call them” (Gen 2:19). From this it is seen that the animals were not made by the Creator’s hand, for the earth brought forth the beasts and the waters brought forth the birds. This is what Scripture wished to show by saying, “formed out of the ground,” for from the joining of earth and water there came forth all the beasts, creeping things, cattle, and birds.

The words, “he brought them unto Adam,” show Adam’s wisdom and the peace that was between the animals and man before man transgressed the commandment. They gathered to man as to a shepherd full of love, without fear; they passed before him in herds according to their kinds and species, neither fearing him nor trembling one before another. First came the flock of harmful animals, after which followed without fear the ranks of harmless animals. Thus Adam, having received authority over the earth, was made lord of all on the same day on which he received the blessing. The creative Word became fact, and the blessing was indeed fulfilled: on that same day man was made lord of all that exists, though he himself soon proved disobedient to the Lord of all. God gave to man not only the dominion over all that was promised, but added also the naming of the animals, which He had not promised. If God granted man more than was promised, would He have denied him what was promised, if man had not sinned?

It is not impossible for man to invent a few names and to keep them in memory; but to invent thousands of names in a single hour, and not to give to the last of those named the name of the first, exceeds the powers of human nature and is hard for him. Man could give many names to many kinds of creeping things, beasts, cattle, and birds, but not to give one kind the name of another is already a work of God; and if it was done by man, then this was given him from God. If God granted man dominion, made him a partaker in creation, clothed him with glory, gave him the garden of Eden — what else remained to be done, and what was not done, that man might carefully keep the commandment? Having spoken of the creation of the animals, of the names given them by Adam, Moses turns to the account of his sleep and how a rib was taken from Adam and from it a wife was made, and says: “But for Adam there was not found a helper meet for him” (Gen 2:20). By the name “helper” is meant Eve. Although the beasts and cattle became helpers to man, a helper out of his own kind would be more fitting. And Eve, besides the care of household affairs and the tending of sheep, oxen, and other herds, could, so far as possible, be a helper to her husband in building, weaving, and other crafts. Although the animals were in subjection to man, in this they could give him no help. Therefore God created for him such a helper as would have, together with him, care of all things, and would in many things assist him.

“And God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh instead thereof. And the rib, which the Lord God had taken from Adam, made he a woman, and brought her unto Adam” (Gen 2:21–22). The man, who until then had been awake, taking pleasure in the radiance of light and knowing nothing of rest, now, stripped bare, lay stretched upon the earth and gave himself to sleep. It is likely that in his sleep Adam saw what was then being done to him. When in the blink of an eye the rib was drawn out, and just as instantly flesh took its place; when the bared bone took on the full appearance and all the beauty of the woman, then God brought her and presented her to Adam.

Narrating Adam’s sleep and how the rib was taken from him, the wife made from it and brought to Adam, Moses writes: “And Adam said: This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called woman, because she was taken out of her man” (Gen 2:23). “This is now” — that is, this one, who came to me after the animals, is not like them; they came forth from the earth, but she is “bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh.” Adam spoke thus either prophetically or, as we have noted above, from a vision in sleep. And just as on that day all the animals received from Adam their names according to their kinds, so also the bone made into a wife he called not by her own proper name — Eve — but by the name “woman,” belonging to the whole kind. The words, “Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife” (Gen 2:24), are spoken to signify that the two who are joined form such an inseparable union as there was in the beginning.

Then Moses says: “And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed” (Gen 2:25). They were not ashamed, not as those who knew not what shame is — for if they had been children, as some say, Scripture would not have said they were naked and not ashamed. Nor would it have said, “the man and his wife,” if they had been not of full age. The names given by Adam sufficiently assure us of his wisdom. The saying, “to till and to keep paradise,” gives us to know his bodily strength. And the commandment given to the first parents bears witness to their mature age; the transgression of the commandment shows their pride. They were not ashamed because they were clothed with glory. But when after the transgression of the commandment that glory had been taken from them, they were ashamed that they had become naked, and with haste they both rushed to cover with leaves, not their bodies, but their shame.
* In Scripture, instead of this word, “eastward” is written. — Editors of Azbyka very.
